EvaluationRules.Message: Stale computer accounts found - not authenticated in 90+ days
Stale computer account check reported issues

Checks.Help: Detects computer accounts that have not updated their password in over 90 days, indicating the computer is decommissioned or off the network. Stale computer accounts inflate your attack surface.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Verify the computer is truly decommissioned before disabling. 2. Disable stale computer accounts: Disable-ADComputer -Identity <computer>. 3. Move disabled accounts to a 'Disabled' OU for 30 days then delete. 4. Automate cleanup with: Search-ADAccount -AccountInactive -TimeSpan 90.00:00:00 -ComputersOnly.

EvaluationRules.Message: Privileged accounts missing AdminSDHolder protection
Privileged accounts not in Protected Users security group
Privileged account security issue detected

Checks.Help: Audits membership of highly privileged AD groups (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, etc.). Over-populated admin groups significantly increase the blast radius of a credential compromise.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Domain Admins should have fewer than 5 members for most environments. 2. Schema Admins and Enterprise Admins should be EMPTY except when actively needed. 3. Use tiered administration model - separate accounts for tier 0/1/2 access. 4. Enable Protected Users security group for all admin accounts. 5. Implement Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs) for admin tasks.

Checks.PassMessage: Krbtgt password has been rotated recently
Checks.FailMessage: Krbtgt password has not been rotated recently

EvaluationRules.Message: Krbtgt password has not been changed in over a year - Golden Ticket risk
Krbtgt password age exceeds recommended rotation period
Krbtgt password age issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks the age of the krbtgt account password. The krbtgt account is used to sign all Kerberos tickets - a compromised krbtgt enables Golden Ticket attacks that can persist indefinitely.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Reset krbtgt password twice (waiting for replication between resets) to invalidate all existing tickets. 2. Use the Microsoft krbtgt account reset script from GitHub (microsoft/New-KrbtgtKeys.ps1). 3. Schedule krbtgt password rotation every 180 days as part of your security maintenance. 4. After reset, monitor for authentication failures during the 10-hour ticket lifetime.

Checks.PassMessage: DSRM passwords are set and current on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: DSRM password issues detected on domain controllers

EvaluationRules.Message: DSRM password has never been set - DC recovery will fail
DSRM password has not been changed in over a year
DSRM password issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks the age of the Directory Services Restore Mode password on each DC. DSRM is the local administrator account for DCs - an unknown or old DSRM password prevents emergency DC recovery.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Reset DSRM password on each DC: ntdsutil 'set dsrm password' 'reset password on server <DC>' quit quit. 2. Store DSRM passwords securely in a password manager or privileged access workstation. 3. Test DSRM access annually during maintenance windows. 4. Consider using LAPS for DSRM password management (requires Windows Server 2019+).

Checks.PassMessage: No excessive stale user accounts detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Large number of user accounts inactive for 180+ days
Significant number of user accounts inactive for 90+ days
Stale user account issue detected

Checks.Help: Identifies user accounts that have not logged in for an extended period (90+ days). Stale accounts are high-value targets for attackers as they are often unmonitored while retaining full access.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Disable accounts inactive for 90+ days: Disable-ADAccount -Identity <user>. 2. Set an account expiration date for contractors and temporary staff. 3. Implement an automated account review process using scheduled tasks or Microsoft Identity Manager. 4. After 180 days of inactivity, delete accounts following your offboarding procedure.

Checks.FailMessage: Password policy does not meet security requirements

EvaluationRules.Message: Password policy security issues detected
Password policy configuration warnings found
Reversible encryption is enabled - passwords stored insecurely
Password policy issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks the Default Domain Password Policy settings against security best practices including minimum length, complexity, and lockout settings. Weak password policies are a primary enabler of credential-based attacks.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Set minimum password length to 14+ characters (NIST recommends 15+). 2. Enable password complexity requirements. 3. Set maximum password age to 365 days or use Fine-Grained Password Policies for admins. 4. Configure account lockout: threshold 10 attempts, lockout duration 15 minutes.

Checks.PassMessage: Account lockout policy meets security requirements

EvaluationRules.Message: Account lockout is not configured - brute force attacks possible
Account lockout policy security issues detected
Account lockout policy configuration warnings found
Account lockout policy issue detected

Checks.Help: Verifies account lockout policy is configured to protect against brute-force password attacks. No lockout policy or very high thresholds allow unlimited password guessing attempts.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Set account lockout threshold to 5-10 failed attempts. 2. Set lockout duration to 15-30 minutes (or until admin unlocks). 3. Set observation window equal to lockout duration. 4. Use Fine-Grained Password Policy (PSO) to set stricter lockout for privileged accounts.

Checks.PassMessage: No accounts with unconstrained delegation detected
Checks.FailMessage: Accounts with unconstrained Kerberos delegation detected

EvaluationRules.Message: User accounts with unconstrained Kerberos delegation detected - impersonation risk
Computer accounts with unconstrained Kerberos delegation detected
Unconstrained delegation configuration issue detected

Checks.Help: Detects accounts configured with unconstrained Kerberos delegation. Unconstrained delegation allows the delegated service to impersonate any user to any service, making it a powerful privilege escalation vector.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Review all flagged accounts - most should not have unconstrained delegation. 2. Change to Constrained Delegation (specify allowed services) if delegation is needed. 3. For modern applications use Resource-Based Constrained Delegation instead. 4. Add sensitive admin accounts to the Protected Users group, which prevents delegation. 5. DCs legitimately have TrustedForDelegation=true - these are expected and should be excluded.

Checks.PassMessage: Kerberos ticket policy meets security requirements

EvaluationRules.Message: Kerberos ticket policy deviates from security recommendations

Checks.Help: Validates Kerberos ticket policy settings in the Default Domain Policy. Misconfigured settings (excessive clock skew tolerance, very long ticket lifetimes) weaken Kerberos security or cause authentication failures.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Open GPMC and edit the Default Domain Policy. 2. Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Account Policies > Kerberos Policy. 3. Recommended settings: Max ticket age 10 hours, Max renew age 7 days, Max service ticket 600 minutes, Max clock skew 5 minutes. 4. Test changes in a non-production environment first - Kerberos policy affects all domain users.

Checks.PassMessage: AdminSDHolder integrity is healthy - no orphaned adminCount accounts or unexpected ACEs

EvaluationRules.Message: Unexpected principal found with Allow permissions on AdminSDHolder object
Accounts with orphaned adminCount=1 detected - no longer in privileged groups but ACL is locked
AdminSDHolder integrity issue detected

Checks.Help: The AdminSDHolder object holds an ACL template that is automatically applied (every 60 minutes by SDProp) to all members of protected groups such as Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and Administrators. Orphaned adminCount=1 accounts are no longer in any privileged group but SDProp no longer corrects their ACL, potentially leaving them as hidden persistence vectors. Unexpected ACEs on the AdminSDHolder itself could allow attackers to grant themselves persistent admin rights.
Checks.Remediation: For orphaned adminCount accounts: 1. Verify the account is not supposed to be in a privileged group. 2. Clear adminCount: Set-ADUser -Identity <account> -Clear adminCount. 3. Restore the default ACL by running: dsacls 'CN=<account>,<DN>' /resetDefaultDACL. For unexpected ACEs on AdminSDHolder: 1. Run Get-Acl 'AD:CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System,<domainDN>' | Select -ExpandProperty Access. 2. Remove unexpected ACEs: (Get-Acl 'AD:CN=AdminSDHolder,...').RemoveAccessRule($ace). 3. Set the modified ACL back: Set-Acl.

Checks.PassMessage: LAPS is deployed with adequate coverage

EvaluationRules.Message: LAPS schema extension not found - LAPS is not deployed in the domain
LAPS coverage is critically low - fewer than 50% of computers have LAPS passwords
LAPS deployment issue detected

Checks.Help: Microsoft Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) randomizes and manages the local Administrator password on domain-joined computers, storing it in AD as a confidential attribute (ms-Mcs-AdmPwd). Without LAPS, local admin passwords are typically the same across all machines, enabling Pass-the-Hash lateral movement from any compromised host. With LAPS, compromising one machine's local admin doesn't help attackers on other machines.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Download LAPS from Microsoft: Install-Module LAPS or download the MSI from Microsoft. 2. Extend the AD schema: Update-AdmPwdADSchema. 3. Set permissions: Set-AdmPwdComputerSelfPermission -OrgUnit <OU>. 4. Configure the LAPS GPO: Computer Config > Admin Templates > LAPS > Enable password management. 5. Deploy the LAPS client MSI to all computers via GPO software installation. 6. Verify: Get-AdmPwdPassword -ComputerName <computer>.

Checks.PassMessage: All Tier-0 privileged accounts are in the Protected Users group
Checks.FailMessage: Privileged accounts not in Protected Users group

EvaluationRules.Message: Privileged accounts found that are not members of Protected Users
Protected Users group issue detected

Checks.Help: The Protected Users security group (introduced in Windows Server 2012 R2) provides additional Kerberos protections for its members: no NTLM authentication, no DES or RC4 encryption, no credential caching, no delegation, and TGTs expire after 4 hours. Adding Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and other Tier-0 accounts to Protected Users significantly reduces the attack surface if a DC is compromised. Note: test compatibility before adding service accounts - they cannot use NTLM or basic delegation.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Test impact in a lab first - Protected Users restrictions break services that rely on NTLM or delegation. 2. Add privileged accounts: Add-ADGroupMember -Identity 'Protected Users' -Members <account>. 3. Prioritize: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and dedicated admin accounts. 4. Do NOT add service accounts to Protected Users unless you have verified they use Kerberos and not NTLM. 5. Monitor for authentication failures after adding accounts.

Checks.PassMessage: All required audit policy subcategories are configured on domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Missing critical audit policy subcategories detected

EvaluationRules.Message: One or more critical audit policy subcategories are not configured on a domain controller
Audit policy configuration issue detected

Checks.Help: Windows Advanced Audit Policy controls which security events are written to the Security event log. Without proper audit configuration, critical security events such as failed logons, privilege use, directory service changes, and account management changes go unlogged. This makes it impossible to detect attacks in progress or reconstruct incidents after the fact. CIS Benchmarks and Microsoft security baselines specify minimum required subcategories for domain controllers.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Configure via Default Domain Controllers Policy GPO: Computer Config > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy. 2. Use auditpol.exe to verify: auditpol /get /category:*. 3. Key categories to enable: Account Logon (Success/Failure), Account Management (Success/Failure), Logon/Logoff (Success/Failure), DS Access (Success), Policy Change (Success), Privilege Use (Success/Failure). 4. Consider deploying Microsoft Security Compliance Toolkit baselines for DCs.

Checks.PassMessage: All service accounts have acceptable permission levels
Checks.FailMessage: Service accounts with excessive permissions detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Kerberoastable service account with sensitive group membership detected - domain compromise risk
Service account with PasswordNeverExpires=true detected - extended Kerberoast cracking window
Service account permission issue detected

Checks.Help: Service accounts with SPNs (Service Principal Names) are vulnerable to Kerberoasting - an offline attack where an attacker requests a Kerberos service ticket and cracks the encrypted ticket offline to recover the account password. If such an account is also a Domain Admin or has other sensitive privileges, cracking the password gives the attacker immediate domain admin access. Service accounts should follow least privilege: only the permissions required for the service, never Domain Admin.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Remove service accounts from privileged groups immediately - create dedicated service accounts with minimum required permissions. 2. For accounts that must be Kerberoastable, use long random passwords (25+ chars): Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity <svc> -Reset -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString -AsPlainText (New-Guid).Guid -Force). 3. Consider using Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) which use 256-bit auto-rotating passwords, eliminating Kerberoast risk: New-ADServiceAccount, Install-ADServiceAccount. 4. Enable Password Never Expires only when absolutely necessary and mitigate with long complex passwords.

EvaluationRules.Message: Computer accounts with both stale logon and stale password detected - high risk accounts
Stale computer accounts detected - not authenticated in 90+ days

Checks.Help: Stale computer accounts (not authenticated in 90+ days) pose security risks: they can be hijacked for lateral movement, may run unpatched software, and inflate your asset inventory. Double-stale accounts (stale logon AND stale password) are particularly risky because they cannot automatically renew their machine password, indicating the computer may be permanently offline but the account remains enabled and usable for Pass-the-Hash or relay attacks.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Disable stale accounts first: Get-ADComputer -Filter {Enabled -eq $true} | where {$_.lastLogonTimestamp -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(-90).ToFileTime()} | Disable-ADAccount. 2. After 30-day observation period, delete confirmed stale accounts. 3. Implement lifecycle process: automated 90-day disable, 180-day deletion. 4. Use AD cleanup tools like AD Tidy or scripts from Get-ADComputer pipeline. 5. Verify with asset management before deleting - some machines have extended offline periods (kiosks, seasonal equipment).

Checks.PassMessage: Schema Admins group has acceptable membership (empty or Administrator only)
Checks.FailMessage: Schema Admins group contains unexpected members

EvaluationRules.Message: Schema Admins contains non-standard members - group should be empty when not performing schema changes
Schema Admins membership issue detected

Checks.Help: The Schema Admins group has unique forest-wide power: members can modify the Active Directory schema, including adding attributes, creating object classes, and changing indexing. Schema changes are permanent and forest-wide - there is no undo. Best practice is to keep Schema Admins completely empty (or contain only the built-in Administrator account) at all times, adding members only when an authorized schema upgrade is in progress, then removing them immediately after. Any persistent membership is a red flag for compromise or misconfiguration.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify all current members: Get-ADGroupMember 'Schema Admins' -Recursive. 2. Remove any non-Administrator members: Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity 'Schema Admins' -Members <account>. 3. If Administrator is the only member, consider removing it too for maximum security. 4. Set up monitoring alerts: anytime Schema Admins membership changes, alert the security team immediately. 5. When schema changes are required, follow a documented process: add account, perform change, remove account, verify removal.
